If none of the optional fields is present, then the\n27: // header value is not matched, only presence of the header is enough.\n28: type HeaderMatch struct {\n29: \t// Mismatch identifies what to do in case there is no match. The default is\n30: \t// to drop the request. Otherwise the overall rule is still considered as\n31: \t// matching, but the mismatches are logged in the access log.\n32: \t//\n33: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Enum=LOG;ADD;DELETE;REPLACE\n34: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n35: \tMismatch MismatchAction `json:\"mismatch,omitempty\"`\n36: \n37: \t// Name identifies the header.\n38: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Required\n39: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:MinLength=1\n40: \tName string `json:\"name\"`\n41: \n42: \t// Secret refers to a secret that contains the value to be matched against.\n43: \t// The secret must only contain one entry. If the referred secret does not\n44: \t// exist, and there is no \"Value\" specified, the match will fail.\n45: \t//\n46: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n47: \tSecret *Secret `json:\"secret,omitempty\"`\n48: \n49: \t// Value matches the exact value of the header. Can be specified either\n50: \t// alone or together with \"Secret\"; will be used as the header value if the\n51: \t// secret can not be found in the latter case.\n52: \t//\n53: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n54: \tValue string `json:\"value,omitempty\"`\n55: }\n56: \n57: // PortRuleHTTP is a list of HTTP protocol constraints. All fields are\n58: // optional, if all fields are empty or missing, the rule does not have any\n59: // effect.\n60: //\n61: // All fields of this type are extended POSIX regex as defined by IEEE Std\n62: // 1003.1, (i.e this follows the egrep/unix syntax, not the perl syntax)\n63: // matched against the path of an incoming request. Currently it can contain\n64: // characters disallowed from the conventional \"path\" part of a URL as defined\n65: // by RFC 3986.\n66: type PortRuleHTTP struct {\n67: \t// Path is an extended POSIX regex matched against the path of a\n68: \t// request. Currently it can contain characters disallowed from the\n69: \t// conventional \"path\" part of a URL as defined by RFC 3986.\n70: \t//\n71: \t// If omitted or empty, all paths are all allowed.\n72: \t//\n73: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n74: \tPath string `json:\"path,omitempty\"`\n75: \n76: \t// Method is an extended POSIX regex matched against the method of a\n77: \t// request, e.g. \"GET\", \"POST\", \"PUT\", \"PATCH\", \"DELETE\", ...\n78: \t//\n79: \t// If omitted or empty, all methods are allowed.\n80: \t//\n81: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n82: \tMethod string `json:\"method,omitempty\"`\n83: \n84: \t// Host is an extended POSIX regex matched against the host header of a\n85: \t// request. Examples:\n86: \t//\n87: \t// - foo.bar.com will match the host fooXbar.com or foo-bar.com\n88: \t// - foo\\.bar\\.com will only match the host foo.bar.com\n89: \t//\n90: \t// If omitted or empty, the value of the host header is ignored.\n91: \t//\n92: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Format=idn-hostname\n93: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n94: \tHost string `json:\"host,omitempty\"`\n95: \n96: \t// Headers is a list of HTTP headers which must be present in the\n97: \t// request. If omitted or empty, requests are allowed regardless of\n98: \t// headers present.\n99: \t//\n100: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n101: \tHeaders []string `json:\"headers,omitempty\"`\n102: \n103: \t// HeaderMatches is a list of HTTP headers which must be\n104: \t// present and match against the given values. Mismatch field can be used\n105: \t// to specify what to do when there is no match.\n106: \t//\n107: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n108: \tHeaderMatches []*HeaderMatch `json:\"headerMatches,omitempty\"`\n109: }\n110: \n111: // Sanitize sanitizes HTTP rules. It ensures that the path and method fields\n112: // are valid regular expressions. Note that the proxy may support a wider-range\n113: // of regular expressions (e.g. that specified by ECMAScript), so this function\n114: // may return some false positives. If the rule is invalid, returns an error.\n115: func (h *PortRuleHTTP) Sanitize() error {\n116: \n117: \tif h.Path != \"\" {\n118: \t\t_, err := regexp.Compile(h.Path)\n119: \t\tif err != nil {\n120: \t\t\treturn err\n121: \t\t}\n122: \t}\n123: \n124: \tif h.Method != \"\" {\n125: \t\t_, err := regexp.Compile(h.Method)\n126: \t\tif err != nil {\n127: \t\t\treturn err\n128: \t\t}\n129: \t}\n130: \n131: \t// Headers are not sanitized.\n132: \n133: \t// But HeaderMatches are\n134: \tfor _, m := range h.HeaderMatches {\n135: \t\tif m.Name == \"\" {\n136: \t\t\treturn fmt.Errorf(\"Header name missing\")\n137: \t\t}\n138: \t\tif m.Mismatch != \"\" \u0026\u0026\n139: \t\t\tm.Mismatch != MismatchActionLog \u0026\u0026 m.Mismatch != MismatchActionAdd \u0026\u0026\n140: \t\t\tm.Mismatch != MismatchActionDelete \u0026\u0026 m.Mismatch != MismatchActionReplace {\n141: \t\t\treturn fmt.Errorf(\"Invalid header action: %s\", m.Mismatch)\n142: \t\t}\n143: \t\tif m.Secret != nil \u0026\u0026 m.Secret.Name == \"\" {\n144: \t\t\treturn fmt.Errorf(\"Secret name missing\")\n145: \t\t}\n146: \t}\n147: \n148: \treturn nil\n149: }\n150: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/cilium--v1.16.5@14bf8b70da6d30733726dd61ffc026d1ed2b187c/-/blob/pkg/policy/api/http.go"}", "annotations": null, "meta": null}], "structuredContent": {"content": "1: // SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
2: // Copyright Authors of Cilium
3: 
4: package api
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"fmt"
8: 	"regexp"
9: )
10: 
11: // MismatchAction specifies what to do when there is no header match
12: // Empty string is the default for making the rule to fail the match.
13: // Otherwise the rule is still considered as matching, but the mismatches
14: // are logged in the access log.
15: type MismatchAction string
16: 
17: const (
18: 	MismatchActionLog     MismatchAction = "LOG"     // Keep checking other matches
19: 	MismatchActionAdd     MismatchAction = "ADD"     // Add the missing value to a possibly multi-valued header
20: 	MismatchActionDelete  MismatchAction = "DELETE"  // Remove the whole mismatching header
21: 	MismatchActionReplace MismatchAction = "REPLACE" // Replace (of add if missing) the header
22: )
23: 
24: // HeaderMatch extends the HeaderValue for matching requirement of a
25: // named header field against an immediate string, a secret value, or
26: // a regex.  If none of the optional fields is present, then the
27: // header value is not matched, only presence of the header is enough.
28: type HeaderMatch struct {
29: 	// Mismatch identifies what to do in case there is no match. The default is
30: 	// to drop the request. Otherwise the overall rule is still considered as
31: 	// matching, but the mismatches are logged in the access log.
32: 	//
33: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Enum=LOG;ADD;DELETE;REPLACE
34: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
35: 	Mismatch MismatchAction `json:"mismatch,omitempty"`
36: 
37: 	// Name identifies the header.
38: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Required
39: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:MinLength=1
40: 	Name string `json:"name"`
41: 
42: 	// Secret refers to a secret that contains the value to be matched against.
43: 	// The secret must only contain one entry. If the referred secret does not
44: 	// exist, and there is no "Value" specified, the match will fail.
45: 	//
46: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
47: 	Secret *Secret `json:"secret,omitempty"`
48: 
49: 	// Value matches the exact value of the header. Can be specified either
50: 	// alone or together with "Secret"; will be used as the header value if the
51: 	// secret can not be found in the latter case.
52: 	//
53: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
54: 	Value string `json:"value,omitempty"`
55: }
56: 
57: // PortRuleHTTP is a list of HTTP protocol constraints. All fields are
58: // optional, if all fields are empty or missing, the rule does not have any
59: // effect.
60: //
61: // All fields of this type are extended POSIX regex as defined by IEEE Std
62: // 1003.1, (i.e this follows the egrep/unix syntax, not the perl syntax)
63: // matched against the path of an incoming request. Currently it can contain
64: // characters disallowed from the conventional "path" part of a URL as defined
65: // by RFC 3986.
66: type PortRuleHTTP struct {
67: 	// Path is an extended POSIX regex matched against the path of a
68: 	// request. Currently it can contain characters disallowed from the
69: 	// conventional "path" part of a URL as defined by RFC 3986.
70: 	//
71: 	// If omitted or empty, all paths are all allowed.
72: 	//
73: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
74: 	Path string `json:"path,omitempty"`
75: 
76: 	// Method is an extended POSIX regex matched against the method of a
77: 	// request, e.g. "GET", "POST", "PUT", "PATCH", "DELETE", ...
78: 	//
79: 	// If omitted or empty, all methods are allowed.
80: 	//
81: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
82: 	Method string `json:"method,omitempty"`
83: 
84: 	// Host is an extended POSIX regex matched against the host header of a
85: 	// request. Examples:
86: 	//
87: 	// - foo.bar.com will match the host fooXbar.com or foo-bar.com
88: 	// - foo\.bar\.com will only match the host foo.bar.com
89: 	//
90: 	// If omitted or empty, the value of the host header is ignored.
91: 	//
92: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Format=idn-hostname
93: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
94: 	Host string `json:"host,omitempty"`
95: 
96: 	// Headers is a list of HTTP headers which must be present in the
97: 	// request. If omitted or empty, requests are allowed regardless of
98: 	// headers present.
99: 	//
100: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
101: 	Headers []string `json:"headers,omitempty"`
102: 
103: 	// HeaderMatches is a list of HTTP headers which must be
104: 	// present and match against the given values. Mismatch field can be used
105: 	// to specify what to do when there is no match.
106: 	//
107: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
108: 	HeaderMatches []*HeaderMatch `json:"headerMatches,omitempty"`
109: }
110: 
111: // Sanitize sanitizes HTTP rules. It ensures that the path and method fields
112: // are valid regular expressions. Note that the proxy may support a wider-range
113: // of regular expressions (e.g. that specified by ECMAScript), so this function
114: // may return some false positives. If the rule is invalid, returns an error.
115: func (h *PortRuleHTTP) Sanitize() error {
116: 
117: 	if h.Path != "" {
118: 		_, err := regexp.Compile(h.Path)
119: 		if err != nil {
120: 			return err
121: 		}
122: 	}
123: 
124: 	if h.Method != "" {
125: 		_, err := regexp.Compile(h.Method)
126: 		if err != nil {
127: 			return err
128: 		}
129: 	}
130: 
131: 	// Headers are not sanitized.
132: 
133: 	// But HeaderMatches are
134: 	for _, m := range h.HeaderMatches {
135: 		if m.Name == "" {
136: 			return fmt.Errorf("Header name missing")
137: 		}
138: 		if m.Mismatch != "" &&
139: 			m.Mismatch != MismatchActionLog && m.Mismatch != MismatchActionAdd &&
140: 			m.Mismatch != MismatchActionDelete && m.Mismatch != MismatchActionReplace {
141: 			return fmt.Errorf("Invalid header action: %s", m.Mismatch)
142: 		}
143: 		if m.Secret != nil && m.Secret.Name == "" {
144: 			return fmt.Errorf("Secret name missing")
145: 		}
146: 	}
147: 
148: 	return nil
149: }

{"meta": null, "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "{"content":"1: // SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0\n2: // Copyright Authors of Cilium\n3: \n4: package api\n5: \n6: import (\n7: \t\"github.com/cilium/proxy/pkg/policy/api/kafka\"\n8: )\n9: \n10: // L4Proto is a layer 4 protocol name\n11: type L4Proto string\n12: \n13: const (\n14: \t// Keep pkg/u8proto up-to-date with any additions here\n15: \n16: \tProtoTCP    L4Proto = \"TCP\"\n17: \tProtoUDP    L4Proto = \"UDP\"\n18: \tProtoSCTP   L4Proto = \"SCTP\"\n19: \tProtoICMP   L4Proto = \"ICMP\"\n20: \tProtoICMPv6 L4Proto = \"ICMPV6\"\n21: \tProtoAny    L4Proto = \"ANY\"\n22: \n23: \tPortProtocolAny = \"0/ANY\"\n24: )\n25: \n26: // IsAny returns true if an L4Proto represents ANY protocol\n27: func (l4 L4Proto) IsAny() bool {\n28: \treturn l4 == ProtoAny || string(l4) == \"\"\n29: }\n30: \n31: // PortProtocol specifies an L4 port with an optional transport protocol\n32: type PortProtocol struct {\n33: \t// Port can be an L4 port number, or a name in the form of \"http\"\n34: \t// or \"http-8080\".\n35: \t//\n36: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Pattern=`^(6553[0-5]|655[0-2][0-9]|65[0-4][0-9]{2}|6[0-4][0-9]{3}|[1-5][0-9]{4}|[0-9]{1,4})|([a-zA-Z0-9]-?)*[a-zA-Z](-?[a-zA-Z0-9])*$`\n37: \tPort string `json:\"port\"`\n38: \n39: \t// EndPort can only be an L4 port number.\n40: \t//\n41: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Minimum=0\n42: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Maximum=65535\n43: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n44: \tEndPort int32 `json:\"endPort,omitempty\"`\n45: \n46: \t// Protocol is the L4 protocol. If omitted or empty, any protocol\n47: \t// matches. Accepted values: \"TCP\", \"UDP\", \"SCTP\", \"ANY\"\n48: \t//\n49: \t// Matching on ICMP is not supported.\n50: \t//\n51: \t// Named port specified for a container may narrow this down, but may not\n52: \t// contradict this.\n53: \t//\n54: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Enum=TCP;UDP;SCTP;ANY\n55: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n56: \tProtocol L4Proto `json:\"protocol,omitempty\"`\n57: }\n58: \n59: // Covers returns true if the ports and protocol specified in the received\n60: // PortProtocol are equal to or a superset of the ports and protocol in 'other'.\n61: // Named ports only cover other named ports with exactly the same name.\n62: func (p PortProtocol) Covers(other PortProtocol) bool {\n63: \tif p.Port != other.Port {\n64: \t\treturn false\n65: \t}\n66: \tif p.Protocol != other.Protocol {\n67: \t\treturn p.Protocol.IsAny()\n68: \t}\n69: \treturn true\n70: }\n71: \n72: // Secret is a reference to a secret, backed by k8s or local file system.\n73: type Secret struct {\n74: \t// Namespace is the namespace in which the secret exists. Context of use\n75: \t// determines the default value if left out (e.g., \"default\").\n76: \t//\n77: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n78: \tNamespace string `json:\"namespace,omitempty\"`\n79: \n80: \t// Name is the name of the secret.\n81: \t//\n82: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Required\n83: \tName string `json:\"name\"`\n84: }\n85: \n86: // TLSContext provides TLS configuration via reference to either k8s secrets\n87: // or via filepath. If both are set, directory is given priority over\n88: // k8sSecrets.\n89: type TLSContext struct {\n90: \t// Secret is the secret that contains the certificates and private key for\n91: \t// the TLS context.\n92: \t// By default, Cilium will search in this secret for the following items:\n93: \t//  - 'ca.crt'  - Which represents the trusted CA to verify remote source.\n94: \t//  - 'tls.crt' - Which represents the public key certificate.\n95: \t//  - 'tls.key' - Which represents the private key matching the public key\n96: \t//                certificate.\n97: \t//\n98: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Required\n99: \tSecret *Secret `json:\"secret\"`\n100: \n101: \t// TrustedCA is the file name or k8s secret item name for the trusted CA.\n102: \t// If omitted, 'ca.crt' is assumed, if it exists. If given, the item must\n103: \t// exist.\n104: \t//\n105: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n106: \tTrustedCA string `json:\"trustedCA,omitempty\"`\n107: \n108: \t// Certificate is the file name or k8s secret item name for the certificate\n109: \t// chain. If omitted, 'tls.crt' is assumed, if it exists. If given, the\n110: \t// item must exist.\n111: \t//\n112: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n113: \tCertificate string `json:\"certificate,omitempty\"`\n114: \n115: \t// PrivateKey is the file name or k8s secret item name for the private key\n116: \t// matching the certificate chain. If omitted, 'tls.key' is assumed, if it\n117: \t// exists. If given, the item must exist.\n118: \t//\n119: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n120: \tPrivateKey string `json:\"privateKey,omitempty\"`\n121: }\n122: \n123: // EnvoyConfig defines a reference to a CiliumEnvoyConfig or CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig\n124: type EnvoyConfig struct {\n125: \t// Kind is the resource type being referred to. Defaults to CiliumEnvoyConfig or\n126: \t// CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig for CiliumNetworkPolicy and CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy,\n127: \t// respectively. The only case this is currently explicitly needed is when referring to a\n128: \t// CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig from CiliumNetworkPolicy, as using a namespaced listener\n129: \t// from a cluster scoped policy is not allowed.\n130: \t//\n131: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Enum=CiliumEnvoyConfig;CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig\n132: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n133: \tKind string `json:\"kind\"`\n134: \n135: \t// Name is the resource name of the CiliumEnvoyConfig or CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig where\n136: \t// the listener is defined in.\n137: \t//\n138: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:MinLength=1\n139: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Required\n140: \tName string `json:\"name\"`\n141: }\n142: \n143: // Listener defines a reference to an Envoy listener specified in a CEC or CCEC resource.\n144: type Listener struct {\n145: \t// EnvoyConfig is a reference to the CEC or CCEC resource in which\n146: \t// the listener is defined.\n147: \t//\n148: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Required\n149: \tEnvoyConfig *EnvoyConfig `json:\"envoyConfig\"`\n150: \n151: \t// Name is the name of the listener.\n152: \t//\n153: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:MinLength=1\n154: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Required\n155: \tName string `json:\"name\"`\n156: \n157: \t// Priority for this Listener that is used when multiple rules would apply different\n158: \t// listeners to a policy map entry. Behavior of this is implementation dependent.\n159: \t//\n160: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Minimum=1\n161: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Maximum=100\n162: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n163: \tPriority uint16 `json:\"priority\"`\n164: }\n165: \n166: // PortRule is a list of ports/protocol combinations with optional Layer 7\n167: // rules which must be met.\n168: type PortRule struct {\n169: \t// Ports is a list of L4 port/protocol\n170: \t//\n171: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n172: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:MaxItems=40\n173: \tPorts []PortProtocol `json:\"ports,omitempty\"`\n174: \n175: \t// TerminatingTLS is the TLS context for the connection terminated by\n176: \t// the L7 proxy.  For egress policy this specifies the server-side TLS\n177: \t// parameters to be applied on the connections originated from the local\n178: \t// endpoint and terminated by the L7 proxy. For ingress policy this specifies\n179: \t// the server-side TLS parameters to be applied on the connections\n180: \t// originated from a remote source and terminated by the L7 proxy.\n181: \t//\n182: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n183: \tTerminatingTLS *TLSContext `json:\"terminatingTLS,omitempty\"`\n184: \n185: \t// OriginatingTLS is the TLS context for the connections originated by\n186: \t// the L7 proxy.  For egress policy this specifies the client-side TLS\n187: \t// parameters for the upstream connection originating from the L7 proxy\n188: \t// to the remote destination. For ingress policy this specifies the\n189: \t// client-side TLS parameters for the connection from the L7 proxy to\n190: \t// the local endpoint.\n191: \t//\n192: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n193: \tOriginatingTLS *TLSContext `json:\"originatingTLS,omitempty\"`\n194: \n195: \t// ServerNames is a list of allowed TLS SNI values. If not empty, then\n196: \t// TLS must be present and one of the provided SNIs must be indicated in the\n197: \t// TLS handshake.\n198: \t//\n199: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n200: \tServerNames []string `json:\"serverNames,omitempty\"`\n201: \n202: \t// listener specifies the name of a custom Envoy listener to which this traffic should be\n203: \t// redirected to.\n204: \t//\n205: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n206: \tListener *Listener `json:\"listener,omitempty\"`\n207: \n208: \t// Rules is a list of additional port level rules which must be met in\n209: \t// order for the PortRule to allow the traffic. If omitted or empty,\n210: \t// no layer 7 rules are enforced.\n211: \t//\n212: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n213: \tRules *L7Rules `json:\"rules,omitempty\"`\n214: }\n215: \n216: // GetPortProtocols returns the Ports field of the PortRule.\n217: func (pd PortRule) GetPortProtocols() []PortProtocol {\n218: \treturn pd.Ports\n219: }\n220: \n221: // GetPortRule returns the PortRule.\n222: func (pd *PortRule) GetPortRule() *PortRule {\n223: \treturn pd\n224: }\n225: \n226: // PortDenyRule is a list of ports/protocol that should be used for deny\n227: // policies. This structure lacks the L7Rules since it's not supported in deny\n228: // policies.\n229: type PortDenyRule struct {\n230: \t// Ports is a list of L4 port/protocol\n231: \t//\n232: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n233: \tPorts []PortProtocol `json:\"ports,omitempty\"`\n234: }\n235: \n236: // GetPortProtocols returns the Ports field of the PortDenyRule.\n237: func (pd PortDenyRule) GetPortProtocols() []PortProtocol {\n238: \treturn pd.Ports\n239: }\n240: \n241: // GetPortRule returns nil has it is not a PortRule.\n242: func (pd *PortDenyRule) GetPortRule() *PortRule {\n243: \treturn nil\n244: }\n245: \n246: // L7Rules is a union of port level rule types. Mixing of different port\n247: // level rule types is disallowed, so exactly one of the following must be set.\n248: // If none are specified, then no additional port level rules are applied.\n249: type L7Rules struct {\n250: \t// HTTP specific rules.\n251: \t//\n252: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n253: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf\n254: \tHTTP []PortRuleHTTP `json:\"http,omitempty\"`\n255: \n256: \t// Kafka-specific rules.\n257: \t//\n258: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n259: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf\n260: \tKafka []kafka.PortRule `json:\"kafka,omitempty\"`\n261: \n262: \t// DNS-specific rules.\n263: \t//\n264: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n265: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf\n266: \tDNS []PortRuleDNS `json:\"dns,omitempty\"`\n267: \n268: \t// Name of the L7 protocol for which the Key-value pair rules apply.\n269: \t//\n270: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n271: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf\n272: \tL7Proto string `json:\"l7proto,omitempty\"`\n273: \n274: \t// Key-value pair rules.\n275: \t//\n276: \t// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional\n277: \tL7 []PortRuleL7 `json:\"l7,omitempty\"`\n278: }\n279: \n280: // Len returns the total number of rules inside `L7Rules`.\n281: // Returns 0 if nil.\n282: func (rules *L7Rules) Len() int {\n283: \tif rules == nil {\n284: \t\treturn 0\n285: \t}\n286: \treturn len(rules.HTTP) + len(rules.Kafka) + len(rules.DNS) + len(rules.L7)\n287: }\n288: \n289: // IsEmpty returns whether the `L7Rules` is nil or contains no rules.\n290: func (rules *L7Rules) IsEmpty() bool {\n291: \treturn rules.Len() == 0\n292: }\n293: \n294: // PortRules is a slice of PortRule.\n295: type PortRules []PortRule\n296: \n297: // Iterate iterates over all elements of PortRules.\n298: func (pr PortRules) Iterate(f func(pr Ports) error) error {\n299: \tfor i := range pr {\n300: \t\terr := f(\u0026pr[i])\n301: \t\tif err != nil {\n302: \t\t\treturn err\n303: \t\t}\n304: \t}\n305: \treturn nil\n306: }\n307: \n308: // Len returns the length of the elements of PortRules.\n309: func (pr PortRules) Len() int {\n310: \treturn len(pr)\n311: }\n312: \n313: // PortDenyRules is a slice of PortDenyRule.\n314: type PortDenyRules []PortDenyRule\n315: \n316: // Iterate iterates over all elements of PortDenyRules.\n317: func (pr PortDenyRules) Iterate(f func(pr Ports) error) error {\n318: \tfor i := range pr {\n319: \t\terr := f(\u0026pr[i])\n320: \t\tif err != nil {\n321: \t\t\treturn err\n322: \t\t}\n323: \t}\n324: \treturn nil\n325: }\n326: \n327: // Len returns the length of the elements of PortDenyRules.\n328: func (pr PortDenyRules) Len() int {\n329: \treturn len(pr)\n330: }\n331: \n332: // Ports is an interface that should be used by all implementations of the\n333: // PortProtocols.\n334: type Ports interface {\n335: \t// GetPortProtocols returns the slice PortProtocol\n336: \tGetPortProtocols() []PortProtocol\n337: \t// GetPortRule returns a PortRule, if the implementation does not support\n338: \t// it, then returns nil.\n339: \tGetPortRule() *PortRule\n340: }\n341: \n342: // PortsIterator is an interface that should be implemented by structures that\n343: // can iterate over a list of Ports interfaces.\n344: type PortsIterator interface {\n345: \tIterate(f func(pr Ports) error) error\n346: \tLen() int\n347: }\n348: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/cilium--v1.16.5@14bf8b70da6d30733726dd61ffc026d1ed2b187c/-/blob/pkg/policy/api/l4.go"}", 4: package api
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"github.com/cilium/proxy/pkg/policy/api/kafka"
8: )
9: 
10: // L4Proto is a layer 4 protocol name
11: type L4Proto string
12: 
13: const (
14: 	// Keep pkg/u8proto up-to-date with any additions here
15: 
16: 	ProtoTCP    L4Proto = "TCP"
17: 	ProtoUDP    L4Proto = "UDP"
18: 	ProtoSCTP   L4Proto = "SCTP"
19: 	ProtoICMP   L4Proto = "ICMP"
20: 	ProtoICMPv6 L4Proto = "ICMPV6"
21: 	ProtoAny    L4Proto = "ANY"
22: 
23: 	PortProtocolAny = "0/ANY"
24: )
25: 
26: // IsAny returns true if an L4Proto represents ANY protocol
27: func (l4 L4Proto) IsAny() bool {
28: 	return l4 == ProtoAny || string(l4) == ""
29: }
30: 
31: // PortProtocol specifies an L4 port with an optional transport protocol
32: type PortProtocol struct {
33: 	// Port can be an L4 port number, or a name in the form of "http"
34: 	// or "http-8080".
35: 	//
36: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Pattern=`^(6553[0-5]|655[0-2][0-9]|65[0-4][0-9]{2}|6[0-4][0-9]{3}|[1-5][0-9]{4}|[0-9]{1,4})|([a-zA-Z0-9]-?)*[a-zA-Z](-?[a-zA-Z0-9])*$`
37: 	Port string `json:"port"`
38: 
39: 	// EndPort can only be an L4 port number.
40: 	//
41: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Minimum=0
42: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Maximum=65535
43: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
44: 	EndPort int32 `json:"endPort,omitempty"`
45: 
46: 	// Protocol is the L4 protocol. If omitted or empty, any protocol
47: 	// matches. Accepted values: "TCP", "UDP", "SCTP", "ANY"
48: 	//
49: 	// Matching on ICMP is not supported.
50: 	//
51: 	// Named port specified for a container may narrow this down, but may not
52: 	// contradict this.
53: 	//
54: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Enum=TCP;UDP;SCTP;ANY
55: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
56: 	Protocol L4Proto `json:"protocol,omitempty"`
57: }
58: 
59: // Covers returns true if the ports and protocol specified in the received
60: // PortProtocol are equal to or a superset of the ports and protocol in 'other'.
61: // Named ports only cover other named ports with exactly the same name.
62: func (p PortProtocol) Covers(other PortProtocol) bool {
63: 	if p.Port != other.Port {
64: 		return false
65: 	}
66: 	if p.Protocol != other.Protocol {
67: 		return p.Protocol.IsAny()
68: 	}
69: 	return true
70: }

72: // Secret is a reference to a secret, backed by k8s or local file system.
73: type Secret struct {
74: 	// Namespace is the namespace in which the secret exists. Context of use
75: 	// determines the default value if left out (e.g., "default").
76: 	//
77: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
78: 	Namespace string `json:"namespace,omitempty"`
79: 
80: 	// Name is the name of the secret.
81: 	//
82: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Required
83: 	Name string `json:"name"`
84: }
85: 
86: // TLSContext provides TLS configuration via reference to either k8s secrets
87: // or via filepath. If both are set, directory is given priority over
88: // k8sSecrets.
89: type TLSContext struct {
90: 	// Secret is the secret that contains the certificates and private key for
91: 	// the TLS context.
92: 	// By default, Cilium will search in this secret for the following items:
93: 	//  - 'ca.crt'  - Which represents the trusted CA to verify remote source.
94: 	//  - 'tls.crt' - Which represents the public key certificate.
95: 	//  - 'tls.key' - Which represents the private key matching the public key
96: 	//                certificate.
97: 	//
98: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Required
99: 	Secret *Secret `json:"secret"`
100: 
101: 	// TrustedCA is the file name or k8s secret item name for the trusted CA.
102: 	// If omitted, 'ca.crt' is assumed, if it exists. If given, the item must
103: 	// exist.
104: 	//
105: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
106: 	TrustedCA string `json:"trustedCA,omitempty"`
107: 
108: 	// Certificate is the file name or k8s secret item name for the certificate
109: 	// chain. If omitted, 'tls.crt' is assumed, if it exists. If given, the
110: 	// item must exist.
111: 	//
112: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
113: 	Certificate string `json:"certificate,omitempty"`
114: 
115: 	// PrivateKey is the file name or k8s secret item name for the private key
116: 	// matching the certificate chain. If omitted, 'tls.key' is assumed, if it
117: 	// exists. If given, the item must exist.
118: 	//
119: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
120: 	PrivateKey string `json:"privateKey,omitempty"`
121: }
122: 
123: // EnvoyConfig defines a reference to a CiliumEnvoyConfig or CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig
124: type EnvoyConfig struct {
125: 	// Kind is the resource type being referred to. Defaults to CiliumEnvoyConfig or
126: 	// CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig for CiliumNetworkPolicy and CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy,
127: 	// respectively. The only case this is currently explicitly needed is when referring to a
128: 	// CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig from CiliumNetworkPolicy, as using a namespaced listener
129: 	// from a cluster scoped policy is not allowed.
130: 	//
131: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Enum=CiliumEnvoyConfig;CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig
132: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
133: 	Kind string `json:"kind"`
134: 
135: 	// Name is the resource name of the CiliumEnvoyConfig or CiliumClusterwideEnvoyConfig where
136: 	// the listener is defined in.
137: 	//
138: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:MinLength=1
139: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Required
140: 	Name string `json:"name"`
141: }
142: 
143: // Listener defines a reference to an Envoy listener specified in a CEC or CCEC resource.
144: type Listener struct {
145: 	// EnvoyConfig is a reference to the CEC or CCEC resource in which
146: 	// the listener is defined.
147: 	//
148: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Required
149: 	EnvoyConfig *EnvoyConfig `json:"envoyConfig"`
150: 
151: 	// Name is the name of the listener.
152: 	//
153: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:MinLength=1
154: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Required
155: 	Name string `json:"name"`
156: 
157: 	// Priority for this Listener that is used when multiple rules would apply different
158: 	// listeners to a policy map entry. Behavior of this is implementation dependent.
159: 	//
160: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Minimum=1
161: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Maximum=100
162: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
163: 	Priority uint16 `json:"priority"`
164: }
165: 
166: // PortRule is a list of ports/protocol combinations with optional Layer 7
167: // rules which must be met.
168: type PortRule struct {
169: 	// Ports is a list of L4 port/protocol
170: 	//
171: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
172: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:MaxItems=40
173: 	Ports []PortProtocol `json:"ports,omitempty"`
174: 
175: 	// TerminatingTLS is the TLS context for the connection terminated by
176: 	// the L7 proxy.  For egress policy this specifies the server-side TLS
177: 	// parameters to be applied on the connections originated from the local
178: 	// endpoint and terminated by the L7 proxy. For ingress policy this specifies
179: 	// the server-side TLS parameters to be applied on the connections
180: 	// originated from a remote source and terminated by the L7 proxy.
181: 	//
182: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
183: 	TerminatingTLS *TLSContext `json:"terminatingTLS,omitempty"`
184: 
185: 	// OriginatingTLS is the TLS context for the connections originated by
186: 	// the L7 proxy.  For egress policy this specifies the client-side TLS
187: 	// parameters for the upstream connection originating from the L7 proxy
188: 	// to the remote destination. For ingress policy this specifies the
189: 	// client-side TLS parameters for the connection from the L7 proxy to
190: 	// the local endpoint.
191: 	//
192: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
193: 	OriginatingTLS *TLSContext `json:"originatingTLS,omitempty"`
194: 
195: 	// ServerNames is a list of allowed TLS SNI values. If not empty, then
196: 	// TLS must be present and one of the provided SNIs must be indicated in the
197: 	// TLS handshake.
198: 	//
199: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
200: 	ServerNames []string `json:"serverNames,omitempty"`
201: 
202: 	// listener specifies the name of a custom Envoy listener to which this traffic should be
203: 	// redirected to.
204: 	//
205: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
206: 	Listener *Listener `json:"listener,omitempty"`
207: 
208: 	// Rules is a list of additional port level rules which must be met in
209: 	// order for the PortRule to allow the traffic. If omitted or empty,
210: 	// no layer 7 rules are enforced.
211: 	//
212: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
213: 	Rules *L7Rules `json:"rules,omitempty"`
214: }
215: 
216: // GetPortProtocols returns the Ports field of the PortRule.
217: func (pd PortRule) GetPortProtocols() []PortProtocol {
218: 	return pd.Ports
219: }
220: 
221: // GetPortRule returns the PortRule.
222: func (pd *PortRule) GetPortRule() *PortRule {
223: 	return pd
224: }
225: 
226: // PortDenyRule is a list of ports/protocol that should be used for deny
227: // policies. This structure lacks the L7Rules since it's not supported in deny
228: // policies.
229: type PortDenyRule struct {
230: 	// Ports is a list of L4 port/protocol
231: 	//
232: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
233: 	Ports []PortProtocol `json:"ports,omitempty"`
234: }
235: 
236: // GetPortProtocols returns the Ports field of the PortDenyRule.
237: func (pd PortDenyRule) GetPortProtocols() []PortProtocol {
238: 	return pd.Ports
239: }
240: 
241: // GetPortRule returns nil has it is not a PortRule.
242: func (pd *PortDenyRule) GetPortRule() *PortRule {
243: 	return nil
244: }
245: 
246: // L7Rules is a union of port level rule types. Mixing of different port
247: // level rule types is disallowed, so exactly one of the following must be set.
248: // If none are specified, then no additional port level rules are applied.
249: type L7Rules struct {
250: 	// HTTP specific rules.
251: 	//
252: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
253: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf
254: 	HTTP []PortRuleHTTP `json:"http,omitempty"`
255: 
256: 	// Kafka-specific rules.
257: 	//
258: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
259: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf
260: 	Kafka []kafka.PortRule `json:"kafka,omitempty"`
261: 
262: 	// DNS-specific rules.
263: 	//
264: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
265: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf
266: 	DNS []PortRuleDNS `json:"dns,omitempty"`
267: 
268: 	// Name of the L7 protocol for which the Key-value pair rules apply.
269: 	//
270: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
271: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:OneOf
272: 	L7Proto string `json:"l7proto,omitempty"`
273: 
274: 	// Key-value pair rules.
275: 	//
276: 	// +kubebuilder:validation:Optional
277: 	L7 []PortRuleL7 `json:"l7,omitempty"`
278: }
279: 
280: // Len returns the total number of rules inside `L7Rules`.
281: // Returns 0 if nil.
282: func (rules *L7Rules) Len() int {
283: 	if rules == nil {
284: 		return 0
285: 	}
286: 	return len(rules.HTTP) + len(rules.Kafka) + len(rules.DNS) + len(rules.L7)
287: }
288: 
289: // IsEmpty returns whether the `L7Rules` is nil or contains no rules.
290: func (rules *L7Rules) IsEmpty() bool {
291: 	return rules.Len() == 0
292: }
293: 
294: // PortRules is a slice of PortRule.
295: type PortRules []PortRule
296: 
297: // Iterate iterates over all elements of PortRules.
298: func (pr PortRules) Iterate(f func(pr Ports) error) error {
299: 	for i := range pr {
300: 		err := f(&pr[i])
301: 		if err != nil {
302: 			return err
303: 		}
304: 	}
305: 	return nil
306: }
307: 
308: // Len returns the length of the elements of PortRules.
309: func (pr PortRules) Len() int {
310: 	return len(pr)
311: }
312: 
313: // PortDenyRules is a slice of PortDenyRule.
314: type PortDenyRules []PortDenyRule
315: 
316: // Iterate iterates over all elements of PortDenyRules.
317: func (pr PortDenyRules) Iterate(f func(pr Ports) error) error {
318: 	for i := range pr {
319: 		err := f(&pr[i])
320: 		if err != nil {
321: 			return err
322: 		}
323: 	}
324: 	return nil
325: }
326: 
327: // Len returns the length of the elements of PortDenyRules.
328: func (pr PortDenyRules) Len() int {
329: 	return len(pr)
330: }
331: 
332: // Ports is an interface that should be used by all implementations of the
333: // PortProtocols.
334: type Ports interface {
335: 	// GetPortProtocols returns the slice PortProtocol
336: 	GetPortProtocols() []PortProtocol
337: 	// GetPortRule returns a PortRule, if the implementation does not support
338: 	// it, then returns nil.
339: 	GetPortRule() *PortRule
340: }
341: 
342: // PortsIterator is an interface that should be implemented by structures that
343: // can iterate over a list of Ports interfaces.
344: type PortsIterator interface {
345: 	Iterate(f func(pr Ports) error) error
346: 	Len() int
347: }

{"meta": null, "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "{"content":"450: \tr.wg.Wait()\n451: \tr.reactToRuleUpdates(r.epsToBumpRevision, r.endpointsToRegen, r.newRev, r.upsertPrefixes, r.releasePrefixes)\n452: }\n453: \n454: // reactToRuleUpdates does the following:\n455: //   - regenerate all endpoints in epsToRegen\n456: //   - bump the policy revision of all endpoints not in epsToRegen, but which are\n457: //     in allEps, to revision rev.\n458: //   - wait for the all endpoint regenerations to be _queued_.\n459: //   - upsert or delete CIDR identities to the ipcache, as needed.\n460: func (r *PolicyReactionEvent) reactToRuleUpdates(epsToBumpRevision, epsToRegen *policy.EndpointSet, rev uint64, upsertPrefixes, releasePrefixes []netip.Prefix) {\n461: \tvar enqueueWaitGroup sync.WaitGroup\n462: \n463: \t// Asynchronously remove the CIDRs from the IPCache, potentially\n464: \t// causing release of the corresponding identities if now unused.\n465: \t// We can proceed with policy regeneration for endpoints even without\n466: \t// ensuring that the ipcache is updated because:\n467: \t// - If another policy still selects the CIDR, the corresponding\n468: \t//   identity will remain live due to the other CIDR. Policy update\n469: \t//   is a no-op for that CIDR.\n470: \t// - If the policy being deleted is the last policy referring to this\n471: \t//   CIDR, then the policy rules will be updated to remove the allow\n472: \t//   for the CIDR below. The traffic would begin to be dropped after\n473: \t//   this operation completes regardless of whether the BPF ipcache or\n474: \t//   policymap gets updated first, so the ordering is not consequential.\n475: \tif len(releasePrefixes) != 0 {\n476: \t\tr.d.ipcache.RemovePrefixes(releasePrefixes, r.source, r.resource)\n477: \t}\n478: \n479: \t// Bump revision of endpoints which don't need to be regenerated.\n480: \tepsToBumpRevision.ForEachGo(\u0026enqueueWaitGroup, func(epp policy.Endpoint) {\n481: \t\tif epp == nil {\n482: \t\t\treturn\n483: \t\t}\n484: \t\tepp.PolicyRevisionBumpEvent(rev)\n485: \t})\n486: \n487: \t// Regenerate all other endpoints.\n488: \t//\n489: \t// This recalculates the policy for the endpoints, taking into account\n490: \t// the latest changes from this event. Any references to new CIDRs\n491: \t// will be processed to determine the selectors for those CIDRs and\n492: \t// prepare the SelectorCache for the CIDR identites. However, at this\n493: \t// point the CIDR identities may not yet exist. They'll be created in\n494: \t// ipcache.UpsertPrefixes() below, which will separately update the\n495: \t// SelectorCache and plumb the datapath for the corresponding BPF\n496: \t// policymap and ipcache map entries.\n497: \tregenMetadata := \u0026regeneration.ExternalRegenerationMetadata{\n498: \t\tReason:            \"policy rules added\",\n499: \t\tRegenerationLevel: regeneration.RegenerateWithoutDatapath,\n500: \t}\n501: \tepsToRegen.ForEachGo(\u0026enqueueWaitGroup, func(ep policy.Endpoint) {\n502: \t\tif ep != nil {\n503: \t\t\tswitch e := ep.(type) {\n504: \t\t\tcase *endpoint.Endpoint:\n505: \t\t\t\t// Do not wait for the returned channel as we want this to be\n506: \t\t\t\t// ASync\n507: \t\t\t\te.RegenerateIfAlive(regenMetadata)\n508: \t\t\tdefault:\n509: \t\t\t\tlog.Errorf(\"BUG: endpoint not type of *endpoint.Endpoint, received '%s' instead\", e)\n510: \t\t\t}\n511: \t\t}\n512: \t})\n513: \n514: \tenqueueWaitGroup.Wait()\n515: \n516: \t// Asynchronously allocate identities for new CIDRs and notify the\n517: \t// SelectorCache / Endpoints to do an incremental identity update to\n518: \t// the datapath maps (if necessary).\n519: \tif len(upsertPrefixes) != 0 {\n520: \t\tr.d.ipcache.UpsertPrefixes(upsertPrefixes, r.source, r.resource)\n521: \t}\n522: }\n523: \n524: // PolicyDeleteEvent is a wrapper around deletion of policy rules with a given\n525: // set of labels from the policy repository in the daemon.\n526: type PolicyDeleteEvent struct {\n527: \tlabels labels.LabelArray\n528: \topts   *policy.DeleteOptions\n529: \td      *Daemon\n530: }\n531: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/cilium--v1.16.5@14bf8b70da6d30733726dd61ffc026d1ed2b187c/-/blob/daemon/cmd/policy.go%3FL450-530"}", "annotations": null, "meta": null}], "structuredContent": {"content": "450: 	r.wg.Wait()
451: 	r.reactToRuleUpdates(r.epsToBumpRevision, r.endpointsToRegen, r.newRev, r.upsertPrefixes, r.releasePrefixes)
452: }
453: 
454: // reactToRuleUpdates does the following:
455: //   - regenerate all endpoints in epsToRegen
456: //   - bump the policy revision of all endpoints not in epsToRegen, but which are
457: //     in allEps, to revision rev.
458: //   - wait for the all endpoint regenerations to be _queued_.
459: //   - upsert or delete CIDR identities to the ipcache, as needed.
460: func (r *PolicyReactionEvent) reactToRuleUpdates(epsToBumpRevision, epsToRegen *policy.EndpointSet, rev uint64, upsertPrefixes, releasePrefixes []netip.Prefix) {
461: 	var enqueueWaitGroup sync.WaitGroup
462: 
463: 	// Asynchronously remove the CIDRs from the IPCache, potentially
464: 	// causing release of the corresponding identities if now unused.
465: 	// We can proceed with policy regeneration for endpoints even without
466: 	// ensuring that the ipcache is updated because:
467: 	// - If another policy still selects the CIDR, the corresponding
468: 	//   identity will remain live due to the other CIDR. Policy update
469: 	//   is a no-op for that CIDR.
470: 	// - If the policy being deleted is the last policy referring to this
471: 	//   CIDR, then the policy rules will be updated to remove the allow
472: 	//   for the CIDR below. The traffic would begin to be dropped after
473: 	//   this operation completes regardless of whether the BPF ipcache or
474: 	//   policymap gets updated first, so the ordering is not consequential.
475: 	if len(releasePrefixes) != 0 {
476: 		r.d.ipcache.RemovePrefixes(releasePrefixes, r.source, r.resource)
477: 	}
478: 
479: 	// Bump revision of endpoints which don't need to be regenerated.
480: 	epsToBumpRevision.ForEachGo(&enqueueWaitGroup, func(epp policy.Endpoint) {
481: 		if epp == nil {
482: 			return
483: 		}
484: 		epp.PolicyRevisionBumpEvent(rev)
485: 	})
486: 
487: 	// Regenerate all other endpoints.
488: 	//
489: 	// This recalculates the policy for the endpoints, taking into account
490: 	// the latest changes from this event. Any references to new CIDRs
491: 	// will be processed to determine the selectors for those CIDRs and
492: 	// prepare the SelectorCache for the CIDR identites. However, at this
493: 	// point the CIDR identities may not yet exist. They'll be created in
494: 	// ipcache.UpsertPrefixes() below, which will separately update the
495: 	// SelectorCache and plumb the datapath for the corresponding BPF
496: 	// policymap and ipcache map entries.
497: 	regenMetadata := &regeneration.ExternalRegenerationMetadata{
498: 		Reason:            "policy rules added",
499: 		RegenerationLevel: regeneration.RegenerateWithoutDatapath,
500: 	}
501: 	epsToRegen.ForEachGo(&enqueueWaitGroup, func(ep policy.Endpoint) {
502: 		if ep != nil {
503: 			switch e := ep.(type) {
504: 			case *endpoint.Endpoint:
505: 				// Do not wait for the returned channel as we want this to be
506: 				// ASync
507: 				e.RegenerateIfAlive(regenMetadata)
508: 			default:
509: 				log.Errorf("BUG: endpoint not type of *endpoint.Endpoint, received '%s' instead", e)
510: 			}
511: 		}
512: 	})
513: 
514: 	enqueueWaitGroup.Wait()
515: 
516: 	// Asynchronously allocate identities for new CIDRs and notify the
517: 	// SelectorCache / Endpoints to do an incremental identity update to
518: 	// the datapath maps (if necessary).
519: 	if len(upsertPrefixes) != 0 {
520: 		r.d.ipcache.UpsertPrefixes(upsertPrefixes, r.source, r.resource)
521: 	}
522: }
523: 
524: // PolicyDeleteEvent is a wrapper around deletion of policy rules with a given
525: // set of labels from the policy repository in the daemon.
526: type PolicyDeleteEvent struct {
527: 	labels labels.LabelArray
528: 	opts   *policy.DeleteOptions
529: 	d      *Daemon
530: }

arguments.file_text:
# Cilium v1.16.5 — Engineering Onboarding Guide

This document answers six orientation questions for engineers joining the Cilium
project. All file paths are relative to the repository root
(`github.com/sg-evals/cilium--v1.16.5`).

---

## 1. Main Entry Point

### Where execution begins

```
daemon/main.go
```

`main()` is a three-liner:

```go
// daemon/main.go
agentHive := hive.New(cmd.Agent)          // build the dependency graph
cmd.Execute(cmd.NewAgentCmd(agentHive))   // parse CLI flags and run
```

### What `NewAgentCmd` does

`daemon/cmd/root.go` constructs a `cobra.Command` whose `RunE` calls
`h.Run()` (the hive lifecycle). Before the command runs, Cobra's
`OnInitialize` hooks fire three setup functions in order:

| Hook | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `option.InitConfig()` | Load config file, config-dir, and env vars via Viper |
| `initDaemonConfig()` | Apply daemon-specific defaults and validate options |
| `initLogging()` | Configure the logrus logger |

### The hive / dependency-injection framework

Cilium uses its own DI framework (`github.com/cilium/hive/cell`). The
top-level cell is defined in `daemon/cmd/cells.go`:

```go
// daemon/cmd/cells.go
var Agent = cell.Module("agent", "Cilium Agent",
    Infrastructure,   // k8s client, resource watchers, metrics, …
    ControlPlane,     // policy, endpoint manager, ipcache, …
    datapath.Cell,    // BPF loader, maps, orchestrator
)
```

Each sub-cell declares its dependencies as constructor parameters; the
framework wires them together at startup and calls `OnStart`/`OnStop`
lifecycle hooks in dependency order.

### Startup sequence (summary)

```
daemon/main.go
  └─ hive.New(cmd.Agent)          ← build DI graph
  └─ cmd.Execute(NewAgentCmd)
       └─ cobra.OnInitialize
            ├─ option.InitConfig()
            ├─ initDaemonConfig()
            └─ initLogging()
       └─ h.Run()                  ← start all cells in order
```

---

## 2. Core Packages

The `pkg/` directory contains ~80 packages. The most important ones for
understanding the agent are:

### Policy engine

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/policy/api/` | Public API types: `Rule`, `IngressRule`, `EgressRule`, `PortRule`, `L7Rules`, `L4Proto`, selectors |
| `pkg/policy/` | In-memory policy repository (`Repository`), compiled L4 filter (`L4Filter`, `L4Policy`), map-state (`MapState`), distillery |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/` | Kubernetes watchers that translate CNP/CCNP/KNP objects into `api.Rules` and call `PolicyAdd` |
| `pkg/policy/directory/` | Watches a local directory for static CNP YAML files |

### Datapath / BPF

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/maps/policymap/` | Go wrapper for the per-endpoint `cilium_policy_<id>` BPF LPM map |
| `pkg/maps/ctmap/` | Connection-tracking BPF maps |
| `pkg/maps/ipcache/` | IP-to-identity BPF map |
| `pkg/maps/lbmap/` | Load-balancer BPF maps |
| `pkg/datapath/linux/` | Linux-specific datapath implementation |
| `pkg/datapath/loader/` | BPF program compilation and loading |

### Endpoint lifecycle

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/endpoint/` | `Endpoint` struct, policy regeneration (`policy.go`), BPF sync (`bpf.go`) |
| `pkg/endpointmanager/` | Registry of all local endpoints |

### Kubernetes integration

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/k8s/` | Kubernetes client, informers, service cache |
| `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/` | CRD Go types: `CiliumNetworkPolicy`, `CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy`, etc. |
| `pkg/k8s/resource/` | Generic typed resource watcher (used by policy watchers) |
| `pkg/k8s/synced/` | Tracks which API groups have been synced |

### Identity and IPCache

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/identity/` | Numeric identity allocation and label-to-identity mapping |
| `pkg/ipcache/` | IP-prefix → identity cache; drives BPF ipcache map updates |

### Configuration and utilities

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/option/` | `DaemonConfig` struct and `InitConfig()` |
| `pkg/labels/` | Label types and selectors |
| `pkg/eventqueue/` | Serialized event queue used for policy reactions |
| `pkg/testutils/` | Test helpers (`PrivilegedTest`, `IntegrationTest`) |

---

## 3. Configuration Loading

### Sources and precedence (highest → lowest)

1. **CLI flags** (cobra/pflag) — e.g. `--enable-ipv6`
2. **Environment variables** — same names with `CILIUM_` prefix and
   hyphens replaced by underscores, e.g. `CILIUM_ENABLE_IPV6`
3. **Config directory** (`--config-dir`) — each file in the directory is
   treated as a key whose content is the value; this is how Kubernetes
   `ConfigMap` mounts are consumed
4. **Config file** (`--config`) — a YAML file; default path is
   `/etc/cilium/cilium-config`

All four sources are unified by **Viper** (`github.com/spf13/viper`).

### Code path

```
option.InitConfig()                    // pkg/option/config.go ~L4215
  ├─ viper.SetEnvPrefix("CILIUM")
  ├─ viper.AutomaticEnv()
  ├─ viper.SetConfigFile(configFile)   // --config flag
  ├─ viper.ReadInConfig()
  └─ for each file in configDir:
       viper.Set(filename, content)    // --config-dir / ConfigMap
```

### The config struct

`option.DaemonConfig` (defined at `pkg/option/config.go` ~L1401) is a
large struct (~500 fields) that holds every tuneable. It is populated
once during startup and then read-only for the lifetime of the process.
Flags are registered in `daemon/cmd/daemon_main.go` via
`flags.StringVar`, `flags.BoolVar`, etc., and bound to Viper with
`viper.BindPFlags`.

### Runtime feature flags

Many features are gated by fields on `DaemonConfig`, for example:

```go
if p.config.EnableK8sNetworkPolicy {
    knpEvents = p.networkPolicies.Events(ctx)
}
```

---

## 4. Test Structure

Cilium uses three distinct test tiers, each with different requirements
and invocation methods.

### Tier 1 — Standard unit tests

Plain `go test ./...`. No special environment needed. These live
alongside the code they test (e.g. `pkg/policy/api/rule_test.go`,
`pkg/policy/repository_test.go`). They use `github.com/stretchr/testify`
for assertions.

```bash
go test ./pkg/policy/...
```

### Tier 2 — Privileged tests (require root / Linux capabilities)

Tests that exercise real network namespaces, BPF maps, or netlink calls
call `testutils.PrivilegedTest(t)` as their first line:

```go
// daemon/cmd/daemon_privileged_test.go
func Test_removeOldRouterState(t *testing.T) {
    testutils.PrivilegedTest(t)   // skips unless PRIVILEGED_TESTS=1
    ...
}
```

The helper (in `pkg/testutils/privileged.go`) skips the test unless the
`PRIVILEGED_TESTS` environment variable is set:

```bash
PRIVILEGED_TESTS=1 go test ./daemon/cmd/...
```

Similarly, integration tests guard themselves with
`testutils.IntegrationTest(t)` and require `INTEGRATION_TESTS=1`.

BPF-level unit tests live in `test/bpf/` and are written in C
(`unit-test.c`), compiled and run via `test/bpf/Makefile`.

### Tier 3 — Control-plane integration tests

Located in `test/controlplane/`, these are full agent integration tests
that spin up a real (but in-process) agent against a fake Kubernetes API
server. They follow a **"k8s objects in, mock datapath state out"**
pattern:

- Test cases register themselves via `init()` calling `suite.AddTestCase`.
- Each test uses `suite.NewControlPlaneTest` to start the agent, then
  applies/deletes Kubernetes objects and asserts on the resulting
  datapath state.
- Many use **golden files** for expected output; update them with
  `-update`:

```bash
go test ./test/controlplane                          # run all
go test ./test/controlplane -test.v -update          # regenerate golden files
go test ./test/controlplane -test.v -debug           # verbose log output
```

### Tier 4 — End-to-end tests

Full cluster tests live in `test/k8s/` and `test/runtime/`. They require
a running Kubernetes cluster (typically provisioned with `kind`) and are
driven by the Ginkgo framework. These are run in CI but not locally
during normal development.

---

## 5. Network Policy Pipeline

A `CiliumNetworkPolicy` (CNP) applied to the cluster travels through
seven stages before it affects packet forwarding.

### Stage 1 — Kubernetes API watch

`pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go` — `policyWatcher.watchResources(ctx)`

A single goroutine selects over four event channels:

| Channel | Source |
|---|---|
| `cnpEvents` | `CiliumNetworkPolicy` resources |
| `ccnpEvents` | `CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy` resources |
| `knpEvents` | Kubernetes native `NetworkPolicy` (if `EnableK8sNetworkPolicy=true`) |
| `cidrGroupEvents` | `CiliumCIDRGroup` resources |

### Stage 2 — Reference resolution

`pkg/policy/k8s/cilium_network_policy.go` — `resolveCiliumNetworkPolicyRefs()`

Before the policy is imported, two types of external references are
inlined:

- **CIDRGroupRef** → expanded to a concrete `CIDRSet` by looking up the
  `CiliumCIDRGroup` cache (`resolveCIDRGroupRef`)
- **ToServices** → translated to `ToCIDRSet` entries using the service
  endpoint cache (`resolveToServices`)

### Stage 3 — Parse to `api.Rules`

`pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/cnp_types.go` — `CiliumNetworkPolicy.Parse()`

The CRD object's `Spec`/`Specs` fields (typed as `*api.Rule` /
`api.Rules`) are validated, namespace-scoped, and returned as a slice of
`*api.Rule`. For native Kubernetes `NetworkPolicy`, the equivalent
function is `pkg/k8s/network_policy.go:ParseNetworkPolicy()`.

### Stage 4 — Policy repository update

`daemon/cmd/policy.go` — `Daemon.PolicyAdd()` → `Daemon.policyAdd()`

`PolicyAdd` is the public entry point (implements the `PolicyManager`
interface). It enqueues a `PolicyAddEvent` onto the daemon's event queue
to serialize concurrent updates. Inside `policyAdd`:

1. Acquires `d.policy.Mutex` (the repository lock).
2. Calls `d.policy.ReplaceByResourceLocked(rules, resourceID)` (for CNP)
   or `d.policy.AddListLocked(rules)` (for gRPC API).
3. Calls `addedRules.FindSelectedEndpoints(...)` to determine which
   endpoints are affected.
4. Releases the lock and enqueues a `PolicyReactionEvent`.

### Stage 5 — Endpoint regeneration

`daemon/cmd/policy.go` — `PolicyReactionEvent.Handle()` →
`reactToRuleUpdates()`

The reaction event:

1. Bumps the policy revision counter for endpoints not directly affected.
2. Calls `endpoint.RegenerateIfAlive(regenMetadata)` for each affected
   endpoint (asynchronously).
3. Calls `ipcache.UpsertPrefixes()` to allocate identities for any new
   CIDR prefixes referenced by the rules.

### Stage 6 — Per-endpoint policy recompilation

`pkg/endpoint/policy.go` — `Endpoint.regeneratePolicy()`

For each affected endpoint, the policy engine:

1. Walks the policy repository and evaluates which rules select this
   endpoint's identity labels.
2. Compiles the matching rules into an `L4Policy` (ingress + egress
   `L4Filter` maps keyed by port/protocol).
3. Expands each `L4Filter` into a `MapState` — a flat map of
   `(identity, port, protocol, direction) → allow/deny`.

### Stage 7 — BPF map synchronization

`pkg/endpoint/bpf.go` — `Endpoint.syncPolicyMap()`

The computed `MapState` is diffed against the currently realized state.
Only the delta is written to the per-endpoint BPF LPM map
(`cilium_policy_<endpointID>`, implemented in
`pkg/maps/policymap/policymap.go`). The BPF datapath then uses this map
for every packet decision via tail-call programs referenced from
`cilium_call_policy` and `cilium_egresscall_policy`.

### Pipeline diagram

```
K8s API server
    │  CiliumNetworkPolicy event
    ▼
pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go          watchResources()
    │  onUpsert / onDelete
    ▼
pkg/policy/k8s/cilium_network_policy.go   resolveCiliumNetworkPolicyRefs()
    │  CIDRGroupRef + ToServices expansion
    ▼
pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/cnp_types.go   CiliumNetworkPolicy.Parse()
    │  → []api.Rule
    ▼
daemon/cmd/policy.go               Daemon.PolicyAdd()
    │  policy repository lock → ReplaceByResourceLocked()
    │  FindSelectedEndpoints()
    ▼
daemon/cmd/policy.go               PolicyReactionEvent.Handle()
    │  endpoint.RegenerateIfAlive()
    │  ipcache.UpsertPrefixes()
    ▼
pkg/endpoint/policy.go             Endpoint.regeneratePolicy()
    │  → MapState (desired)
    ▼
pkg/endpoint/bpf.go                Endpoint.syncPolicyMap()
    │  diff desired vs realized
    ▼
pkg/maps/policymap/policymap.go    cilium_policy_<id> BPF map
    │
    ▼
BPF datapath (packet verdict)
```

---

## 6. Adding a New Network Policy Type

To introduce a new policy type (for example, a hypothetical
`CiliumDNSEgressPolicy` CRD), you need to touch files across four layers.
Below is the complete checklist with the canonical existing type as a
reference.

### Layer 1 — CRD Go types

**Reference:** `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/cnp_types.go`

1. Create `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/<yourtype>_types.go` with the
   Kubernetes object struct (embed `metav1.TypeMeta` + `metav1.ObjectMeta`).
2. Add a `Spec *api.Rule` (or `Specs api.Rules`) field to hold the policy
   rules.
3. Implement a `Parse() (api.Rules, error)` method that validates the
   spec and returns `[]api.Rule`. Use
   `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/utils/utils.go:ParseToCiliumRule()` to
   namespace-scope selectors.
4. Run `make generate` to regenerate `zz_generated.deepcopy.go` and
   `zz_generated.deepequal.go`.

### Layer 2 — API rule types (if adding new match fields)

**Reference:** `pkg/policy/api/l4.go`, `pkg/policy/api/http.go`

If your policy type introduces new match criteria (e.g. a new L7
protocol):

1. Add a new field to `L7Rules` in `pkg/policy/api/l4.go`:
   ```go
   type L7Rules struct {
       HTTP  []PortRuleHTTP  `json:"http,omitempty"`
       Kafka []kafka.PortRule `json:"kafka,omitempty"`
       DNS   []PortRuleDNS   `json:"dns,omitempty"`
       MyProto []PortRuleMyProto `json:"myProto,omitempty"` // new
       ...
   }
   ```
2. Create `pkg/policy/api/myproto.go` with the `PortRuleMyProto` struct
   and a `Sanitize() error` method.
3. Update `L7Rules.Len()` to count the new field.
4. Add a new `L7ParserType` constant in `pkg/policy/l4.go` and handle it
   in the `switch` that assigns `l4.L7Parser` (~line 876).

### Layer 3 — Kubernetes watcher

**Reference:** `pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go`,
`pkg/policy/k8s/cilium_network_policy.go`

1. Add a `resource.Resource[*YourType]` field to `policyWatcher`.
2. In `watchResources()`, subscribe to events for your new resource type
   and add a `case` to the `select` loop.
3. Implement `onUpsertYourType()` and `onDeleteYourType()` handlers
   (following the pattern of `onUpsert` / `onDelete` for CNP):
   - Call `yourObj.Parse()` to get `api.Rules`.
   - Call `p.policyManager.PolicyAdd(rules, &policy.AddOptions{...})`.
4. Add a `yourTypeSynced atomic.Bool` field and set it on the `Sync`
   event.

### Layer 4 — Cell wiring

**Reference:** `pkg/policy/k8s/cell.go`

1. Add your new `resource.Resource[*YourType]` as a parameter to the
   `newPolicyWatcher` constructor so the hive framework injects it.
2. Register the new API group with `k8sAPIGroups.AddAPI(...)` and
   `k8sResourceSynced.BlockWaitGroupToSyncResources(...)` so the agent
   waits for the initial list before declaring itself ready.

### Layer 5 — CRD registration

**Reference:** `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/register.go`

1. Add your new type to the `SchemeBuilder` so it is registered with the
   Kubernetes API machinery.
2. Add the CRD YAML manifest under `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/crds/`.

### Layer 6 — Tests

1. **Unit tests** — add `pkg/policy/api/myproto_test.go` to test
   `Sanitize()` and validation logic.
2. **Watcher tests** — add
   `pkg/policy/k8s/<yourtype>_test.go` following the pattern of
   `cilium_network_policy_test.go`.
3. **Control-plane integration test** — add a new test case under
   `test/controlplane/` using `suite.AddTestCase` and
   `suite.NewControlPlaneTest`. Provide input YAML fixtures and golden
   output files for the expected datapath state.

### Summary of files to create or modify

| File | Action |
|---|---|
| `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/<yourtype>_types.go` | **Create** — CRD struct + `Parse()` |
| `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/register.go` | **Modify** — register new type |
| `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/crds/<yourtype>.yaml` | **Create** — CRD manifest |
| `pkg/policy/api/l4.go` | **Modify** — add field to `L7Rules` (if new L7 protocol) |
| `pkg/policy/api/<yourproto>.go` | **Create** — new `PortRule` struct (if new L7 protocol) |
| `pkg/policy/l4.go` | **Modify** — add `L7ParserType` constant and switch case |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go` | **Modify** — add resource field + event channel |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/<yourtype>.go` | **Create** — `onUpsert` / `onDelete` handlers |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/cell.go` | **Modify** — inject new resource into constructor |
| `test/controlplane/<yourtype>/` | **Create** — integration test fixtures |

# Cilium v1.16.5 — Engineering Onboarding Guide

This document answers six orientation questions for engineers joining the Cilium
project. All file paths are relative to the repository root
(`github.com/sg-evals/cilium--v1.16.5`).

---

## 1. Main Entry Point

### Where execution begins

```
daemon/main.go
```

`main()` is a three-liner:

```go
// daemon/main.go
agentHive := hive.New(cmd.Agent)          // build the dependency graph
cmd.Execute(cmd.NewAgentCmd(agentHive))   // parse CLI flags and run
```

### What `NewAgentCmd` does

`daemon/cmd/root.go` constructs a `cobra.Command` whose `RunE` calls
`h.Run()` (the hive lifecycle). Before the command runs, Cobra's
`OnInitialize` hooks fire three setup functions in order:

| Hook | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `option.InitConfig()` | Load config file, config-dir, and env vars via Viper |
| `initDaemonConfig()` | Apply daemon-specific defaults and validate options |
| `initLogging()` | Configure the logrus logger |

### The hive / dependency-injection framework

Cilium uses its own DI framework (`github.com/cilium/hive/cell`). The
top-level cell is defined in `daemon/cmd/cells.go`:

```go
// daemon/cmd/cells.go
var Agent = cell.Module("agent", "Cilium Agent",
    Infrastructure,   // k8s client, resource watchers, metrics, …
    ControlPlane,     // policy, endpoint manager, ipcache, …
    datapath.Cell,    // BPF loader, maps, orchestrator
)
```

Each sub-cell declares its dependencies as constructor parameters; the
framework wires them together at startup and calls `OnStart`/`OnStop`
lifecycle hooks in dependency order.

### Startup sequence (summary)

```
daemon/main.go
  └─ hive.New(cmd.Agent)          ← build DI graph
  └─ cmd.Execute(NewAgentCmd)
       └─ cobra.OnInitialize
            ├─ option.InitConfig()
            ├─ initDaemonConfig()
            └─ initLogging()
       └─ h.Run()                  ← start all cells in order
```

---

## 2. Core Packages

The `pkg/` directory contains ~80 packages. The most important ones for
understanding the agent are:

### Policy engine

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/policy/api/` | Public API types: `Rule`, `IngressRule`, `EgressRule`, `PortRule`, `L7Rules`, `L4Proto`, selectors |
| `pkg/policy/` | In-memory policy repository (`Repository`), compiled L4 filter (`L4Filter`, `L4Policy`), map-state (`MapState`), distillery |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/` | Kubernetes watchers that translate CNP/CCNP/KNP objects into `api.Rules` and call `PolicyAdd` |
| `pkg/policy/directory/` | Watches a local directory for static CNP YAML files |

### Datapath / BPF

| Package | Role |
|---|---|

| `pkg/maps/ctmap/` | Connection-tracking BPF maps |
| `pkg/maps/ipcache/` | IP-to-identity BPF map |
| `pkg/maps/lbmap/` | Load-balancer BPF maps |
| `pkg/datapath/linux/` | Linux-specific datapath implementation |
| `pkg/datapath/loader/` | BPF program compilation and loading |

### Endpoint lifecycle

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/endpoint/` | `Endpoint` struct, policy regeneration (`policy.go`), BPF sync (`bpf.go`) |
| `pkg/endpointmanager/` | Registry of all local endpoints |

| `pkg/k8s/synced/` | Tracks which API groups have been synced |

### Identity and IPCache

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `pkg/identity/` | Numeric identity allocation and label-to-identity mapping |
| `pkg/ipcache/` | IP-prefix → identity cache; drives BPF ipcache map updates |

### Configuration and utilities

|---|---|

| `pkg/labels/` | Label types and selectors |

## 5. Network Policy Pipeline

### Stage 1 — Kubernetes API watch

`pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go` — `policyWatcher.watchResources(ctx)`

| Channel | Source |
|---|---|
| `cnpEvents` | `CiliumNetworkPolicy` resources |
| `ccnpEvents` | `CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy` resources |

inlined:

- **CIDRGroupRef** → expanded to a concrete `CIDRSet` by looking up the
  `CiliumCIDRGroup` cache (`resolveCIDRGroupRef`)
- **ToServices** → translated to `ToCIDRSet` entries using the service
  endpoint cache (`resolveToServices`)

### Stage 3 — Parse to `api.Rules`

`pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/cnp_types.go` — `CiliumNetworkPolicy.Parse()`

The CRD object's `Spec`/`Specs` fields (typed as `*api.Rule` /
`api.Rules`) are validated, namespace-scoped, and returned as a slice of
`*api.Rule`. For native Kubernetes `NetworkPolicy`, the equivalent
function is `pkg/k8s/network_policy.go:ParseNetworkPolicy()`.

### Stage 4 — Policy repository update

`daemon/cmd/policy.go` — `Daemon.PolicyAdd()` → `Daemon.policyAdd()`

`PolicyAdd` is the public entry point (implements the `PolicyManager`
interface). It enqueues a `PolicyAddEvent` onto the daemon's event queue
to serialize concurrent updates. Inside `policyAdd`:

1. Acquires `d.policy.Mutex` (the repository lock).
2. Calls `d.policy.ReplaceByResourceLocked(rules, resourceID)` (for CNP)
   or `d.policy.AddListLocked(rules)` (for gRPC API).
3. Calls `addedRules.FindSelectedEndpoints(...)` to determine which
   endpoints are affected.
4. Releases the lock and enqueues a `PolicyReactionEvent`.

### Stage 5 — Endpoint regeneration

`daemon/cmd/policy.go` — `PolicyReactionEvent.Handle()` →
`reactToRuleUpdates()`

The reaction event:

1. Bumps the policy revision counter for endpoints not directly affected.
2. Calls `endpoint.RegenerateIfAlive(regenMetadata)` for each affected
   endpoint (asynchronously).
3. Calls `ipcache.UpsertPrefixes()` to allocate identities for any new
   CIDR prefixes referenced by the rules.

### Stage 6 — Per-endpoint policy recompilation

`pkg/endpoint/policy.go` — `Endpoint.regeneratePolicy()`

For each affected endpoint, the policy engine:

1. Walks the policy repository and evaluates which rules select this
   endpoint's identity labels.
2. Compiles the matching rules into an `L4Policy` (ingress + egress
   `L4Filter` maps keyed by port/protocol).
3. Expands each `L4Filter` into a `MapState` — a flat map of
   `(identity, port, protocol, direction) → allow/deny`.

### Stage 7 — BPF map synchronization

`pkg/endpoint/bpf.go` — `Endpoint.syncPolicyMap()`

The computed `MapState` is diffed against the currently realized state.
Only the delta is written to the per-endpoint BPF LPM map
(`cilium_policy_<endpointID>`, implemented in
`pkg/maps/policymap/policymap.go`). The BPF datapath then uses this map
for every packet decision via tail-call programs referenced from
`cilium_call_policy` and `cilium_egresscall_policy`.

### Pipeline diagram

```
K8s API server
    │  CiliumNetworkPolicy event
    ▼
pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go          watchResources()
    │  onUpsert / onDelete
    ▼
pkg/policy/k8s/cilium_network_policy.go   resolveCiliumNetworkPolicyRefs()
    │  CIDRGroupRef + ToServices expansion
    ▼
pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/cnp_types.go   CiliumNetworkPolicy.Parse()
    │  → []api.Rule
    ▼
daemon/cmd/policy.go               Daemon.PolicyAdd()
    │  policy repository lock → ReplaceByResourceLocked()
    │  FindSelectedEndpoints()
    ▼
daemon/cmd/policy.go               PolicyReactionEvent.Handle()
    │  endpoint.RegenerateIfAlive()
    │  ipcache.UpsertPrefixes()
    ▼
pkg/endpoint/policy.go             Endpoint.regeneratePolicy()
    │  → MapState (desired)
    ▼
pkg/endpoint/bpf.go                Endpoint.syncPolicyMap()
    │  diff desired vs realized
    ▼
pkg/maps/policymap/policymap.go    cilium_policy_<id> BPF map
    │
    ▼
BPF datapath (packet verdict)
```

---

## 6. Adding a New Network Policy Type

To introduce a new policy type (for example, a hypothetical
`CiliumDNSEgressPolicy` CRD), you need to touch files across four layers.
Below is the complete checklist with the canonical existing type as a
reference.

### Layer 1 — CRD Go types

**Reference:** `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/cnp_types.go`

1. Create `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/<yourtype>_types.go` with the
   Kubernetes object struct (embed `metav1.TypeMeta` + `metav1.ObjectMeta`).
2. Add a `Spec *api.Rule` (or `Specs api.Rules`) field to hold the policy
   rules.
3. Implement a `Parse() (api.Rules, error)` method that validates the
   spec and returns `[]api.Rule`. Use
   `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/utils/utils.go:ParseToCiliumRule()` to
   namespace-scope selectors.
4. Run `make generate` to regenerate `zz_generated.deepcopy.go` and
   `zz_generated.deepequal.go`.

### Layer 2 — API rule types (if adding new match fields)

**Reference:** `pkg/policy/api/l4.go`, `pkg/policy/api/http.go`

If your policy type introduces new match criteria (e.g. a new L7
protocol):

1. Add a new field to `L7Rules` in `pkg/policy/api/l4.go`:
   ```go
   type L7Rules struct {
       HTTP  []PortRuleHTTP  `json:"http,omitempty"`
       Kafka []kafka.PortRule `json:"kafka,omitempty"`
       DNS   []PortRuleDNS   `json:"dns,omitempty"`
       MyProto []PortRuleMyProto `json:"myProto,omitempty"` // new
       ...
   }
   ```
2. Create `pkg/policy/api/myproto.go` with the `PortRuleMyProto` struct
   and a `Sanitize() error` method.
3. Update `L7Rules.Len()` to count the new field.
4. Add a new `L7ParserType` constant in `pkg/policy/l4.go` and handle it
   in the `switch` that assigns `l4.L7Parser` (~line 876).

### Layer 3 — Kubernetes watcher

**Reference:** `pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go`,
`pkg/policy/k8s/cilium_network_policy.go`

1. Add a `resource.Resource[*YourType]` field to `policyWatcher`.
2. In `watchResources()`, subscribe to events for your new resource type
   and add a `case` to the `select` loop.
3. Implement `onUpsertYourType()` and `onDeleteYourType()` handlers
   (following the pattern of `onUpsert` / `onDelete` for CNP):
   - Call `yourObj.Parse()` to get `api.Rules`.
   - Call `p.policyManager.PolicyAdd(rules, &policy.AddOptions{...})`.
4. Add a `yourTypeSynced atomic.Bool` field and set it on the `Sync`
   event.

### Layer 4 — Cell wiring

**Reference:** `pkg/policy/k8s/cell.go`

1. Add your new `resource.Resource[*YourType]` as a parameter to the
   `newPolicyWatcher` constructor so the hive framework injects it.
2. Register the new API group with `k8sAPIGroups.AddAPI(...)` and
   `k8sResourceSynced.BlockWaitGroupToSyncResources(...)` so the agent
   waits for the initial list before declaring itself ready.

### Layer 5 — CRD registration

**Reference:** `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/register.go`

1. Add your new type to the `SchemeBuilder` so it is registered with the
   Kubernetes API machinery.
2. Add the CRD YAML manifest under `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/crds/`.

### Layer 6 — Tests

1. **Unit tests** — add `pkg/policy/api/myproto_test.go` to test
   `Sanitize()` and validation logic.
2. **Watcher tests** — add
   `pkg/policy/k8s/<yourtype>_test.go` following the pattern of
   `cilium_network_policy_test.go`.
3. **Control-plane integration test** — add a new test case under
   `test/controlplane/` using `suite.AddTestCase` and
   `suite.NewControlPlaneTest`. Provide input YAML fixtures and golden
   output files for the expected datapath state.

### Summary of files to create or modify

| File | Action |
|---|---|
| `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/<yourtype>_types.go` | **Create** — CRD struct + `Parse()` |
| `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/register.go` | **Modify** — register new type |
| `pkg/k8s/apis/cilium.io/v2/crds/<yourtype>.yaml` | **Create** — CRD manifest |
| `pkg/policy/api/l4.go` | **Modify** — add field to `L7Rules` (if new L7 protocol) |
| `pkg/policy/api/<yourproto>.go` | **Create** — new `PortRule` struct (if new L7 protocol) |
| `pkg/policy/l4.go` | **Modify** — add `L7ParserType` constant and switch case |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/watcher.go` | **Modify** — add resource field + event channel |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/<yourtype>.go` | **Create** — `onUpsert` / `onDelete` handlers |
| `pkg/policy/k8s/cell.go` | **Modify** — inject new resource into constructor |
| `test/controlplane/<yourtype>/` | **Create** — integration test fixtures |
